name: Seychelles
tagline: From empty islands to Creole republic
summary: Seychelles is an archipelago of granitic and coral islands in the western Indian Ocean that had no native human population. Sighted by European navigators in the sixteenth century and long a haunt of pirates, it was claimed by France in 1756, settled from 1770 with enslaved Africans on its plantations, and ceded to Britain in 1814. Out of slavery and the later settlement of Africans rescued from slave ships grew a distinct Creole society. Seychelles became independent on 29 June 1976, passed through a 1977 coup and a one-party socialist era, returned to multiparty democracy in 1993, and today is a high-income nation built on tourism and tuna.

founding.label: Independence from the United Kingdom
founding.detail: The Republic of Seychelles became an independent state within the Commonwealth, with James Mancham as its first president and France-Albert René as prime minister.

eras.title: Empty Islands and Pirates
eras.standfirst: Sighted by passing Europeans but settled by none, the islands lay empty for two more centuries, frequented chiefly by pirates.
body: Unlike almost every other archipelago in the Indian Ocean, the granitic and coral islands that became Seychelles had no native human population. Portuguese navigators on the route to India sighted islands of the group during the early sixteenth century; Vasco da Gama's expedition recorded the outlying Amirantes in 1502, and a sighting in 1503 is associated with what was probably Silhouette Island.

The first recorded landing came on 19 January 1609, when men from the English East India Company ship Ascension went ashore. They found the islands wooded, watered and wholly uninhabited, but the Company made no move to settle, and the archipelago was left to itself for another century and a half.

During those decades the scattered islands served as a refuge and watering stop for pirates operating across the Indian Ocean, who preyed on the rich merchant traffic between Europe, Arabia and the East. No permanent community took root, and the islands remained a name on the charts rather than a colony.

events.title: Portuguese navigators sight the islands
events.summary: Vasco da Gama's second voyage to India recorded the Amirantes group; further Portuguese sightings followed in the early 1500s, giving Europe its first knowledge of the archipelago.

events.title: First recorded landing by the Ascension
events.summary: Crew of the English East India Company ship Ascension landed and found the islands fertile and uninhabited, but the Company chose not to settle.

events.title: A haunt of pirates
events.summary: For a century and a half the empty islands were used by pirates as a hideout and watering place while they preyed on Indian Ocean shipping.

pullquote.text: An archipelago without a native people, found by Europe but settled by no one for two hundred years.

eras.title: The French Claim and First Settlers
eras.standfirst: France claimed and named the islands, then planted a settlement of colonists and enslaved Africans on spice and food plantations.
body: French interest grew once Mauritius (Isle de France) became a base for expansion. Expeditions led by Lazare Picault in 1742 and 1744 surveyed the main island and named it Mahé after the governor Bertrand-François Mahé de La Bourdonnais. On 1 November 1756 Corneille Nicholas Morphey laid a stone of possession, claiming the group for France and naming it after Jean Moreau de Séchelles, the French finance minister — the origin of the modern name Seychelles.

Settlement followed on 12 August 1770, when a party arrived to establish the first permanent colony, comprising a small number of French colonists together with enslaved Africans, Indians and others. The naturalist and administrator Pierre Poivre promoted the islands as spice gardens, hoping to break the Dutch monopoly on cloves and nutmeg. Spices were later joined and overtaken by cotton, coconut, maize and cinnamon as plantation crops.

The plantation economy depended on the forced labour of enslaved Africans, who soon outnumbered the European settlers and formed the demographic foundation of the future Seychellois population. By the close of the eighteenth century a small but established colonial society had taken hold around Mahé.

events.title: France claims and names the islands
events.summary: Corneille Nicholas Morphey laid a stone of possession and named the archipelago after finance minister Jean Moreau de Séchelles; the main island was named Mahé.

events.title: First permanent settlement
events.summary: Colonists, enslaved Africans and Indians established the first lasting settlement near Mahé to begin spice cultivation.

events.title: Spice and plantation economy
events.summary: Pierre Poivre promoted spice gardens to challenge the Dutch monopoly; cotton, coconut, maize and cinnamon followed, all worked by enslaved Africans.

figures.name: Pierre Poivre
figures.blurb: Intendant of Isle de France who championed Seychelles as a base for spice cultivation to break the Dutch monopoly on cloves and nutmeg.

eras.title: British Rule and Emancipation
eras.standfirst: Captured during the Napoleonic Wars and ceded in 1814, the islands abolished slavery and absorbed Africans freed from slave ships.
body: British warships repeatedly appeared off the islands during the Napoleonic Wars. The French administrator Jean-Baptiste Quéau de Quincy surrendered the colony several times between 1794 and 1811, each time resuming French administration once the British squadrons departed. The Treaty of Paris of 1814 settled the matter, ceding Seychelles to Britain together with Mauritius; this time the transfer was permanent, and from then until 1903 Seychelles was governed as a dependency of Mauritius.

Britain abolished slavery across its empire, and in Seychelles emancipation took full effect in 1835, freeing several thousand enslaved people who made up most of the population. Slave-trading nonetheless continued in the region, and to suppress it the Royal Navy intercepted Arab dhows and landed those it rescued in Seychelles. Between roughly 1861 and 1874 some two to three thousand of these 'Liberated Africans' were brought ashore, many of their children schooled by missionaries at Venn's Town.

Out of the enslaved population, the liberated Africans and the French planter class grew the Creole society — and the French-based Seychellois Creole language — that defines the islands today. In 1903 Seychelles was detached from Mauritius and made a separate British Crown Colony.

events.title: Capitulations during the Napoleonic Wars
events.summary: Administrator Quéau de Quincy surrendered the islands to British squadrons several times, restoring French rule whenever they left.

events.title: Cession to Britain by the Treaty of Paris
events.summary: Seychelles was formally ceded to Britain with Mauritius and governed thereafter as a dependency of Mauritius.

events.title: Abolition of slavery
events.summary: Emancipation freed the several thousand enslaved Africans who formed the majority of the colony's population.

events.title: Liberated Africans resettled
events.summary: The Royal Navy landed thousands of Africans rescued from intercepted slave dhows, deepening the islands' Creole society.

events.title: Separate Crown Colony
events.summary: Seychelles was detached from Mauritius and constituted as a British Crown Colony in its own right.

figures.name: Jean-Baptiste Quéau de Quincy
figures.blurb: Pragmatic administrator who repeatedly capitulated to British warships during the Napoleonic Wars while preserving the colony's neutrality and French character.

pullquote.text: From the enslaved and the liberated alike rose the Creole people of the islands.

eras.title: An Extraordinary Natural Heritage
eras.standfirst: Two World Heritage sites — Aldabra Atoll and the Vallée de Mai — preserve the giant tortoise and the coco de mer.
body: Seychelles is as renowned for its biology as for its history. The remote Aldabra Atoll, one of the world's largest raised coral atolls, escaped human settlement and shelters the largest population of giant tortoises on Earth, the Aldabra giant tortoise. Its isolation made it a refuge for species that vanished elsewhere in the Indian Ocean.

On the island of Praslin, the Vallée de Mai preserves a near-pristine palm forest dominated by the coco de mer (Lodoicea maldivica), whose distinctive double seed is the largest in the plant kingdom. Once believed to grow on the sea floor, the palm gave rise to centuries of legend before its island home was understood.

UNESCO inscribed Aldabra Atoll as a World Heritage Site in 1982 and the Vallée de Mai in 1983; both are managed by the Seychelles Islands Foundation. Their protection has become central to the country's identity and to its conservation-minded tourism.

events.title: Aldabra Atoll inscribed by UNESCO
events.summary: The raised coral atoll, home to the world's largest giant-tortoise population, was added to the World Heritage List for its outstanding natural value.

events.title: Vallée de Mai inscribed by UNESCO
events.summary: Praslin's palm forest, where the coco de mer grows wild, was recognised as a World Heritage Site for its unique flora.

eras.title: Independence, Coup and One-Party State
eras.standfirst: Independence in 1976 was followed by a 1977 coup, a one-party socialist state and a failed mercenary raid in 1981.
body: Seychelles became an independent republic within the Commonwealth on 29 June 1976. James Mancham of the Seychelles Democratic Party became the first president, with France-Albert René of the Seychelles People's United Party as prime minister, but the two leaders' visions quickly diverged.

On 5 June 1977, while Mancham was abroad, supporters of René seized power in an almost bloodless coup, and René assumed the presidency. A new constitution in 1979 established a one-party socialist state under the Seychelles People's Progressive Front, with nationalised enterprises, a national youth service and curbs on the press. On 25 November 1981 a band of mercenaries led by 'Mad' Mike Hoare, posing as a beer-drinkers' club and backed by elements in South Africa, tried to overthrow René; the plot collapsed at the airport when weapons were found, and the mercenaries hijacked an airliner to escape.

René's government survived further plots and ruled for more than a decade as a single-party socialist regime, aligning Seychelles with the non-aligned and socialist world while building up health, education and welfare services.

events.title: Independence from the United Kingdom
events.summary: Seychelles became an independent republic within the Commonwealth, with James Mancham as first president and France-Albert René as prime minister.

events.title: Coup brings René to power
events.summary: Supporters of France-Albert René overthrew Mancham in an almost bloodless coup while he was abroad; René took the presidency.

events.title: One-party socialist state
events.summary: A new constitution made the Seychelles People's Progressive Front the sole legal party, with nationalisations and restrictions on the press.

events.title: Mike Hoare's mercenary raid fails
events.summary: Mercenaries disguised as a beer-drinkers' club and backed from South Africa tried to seize power; the plot collapsed and they hijacked an airliner to flee.

figures.name: James Mancham
figures.blurb: Pro-Western lawyer and writer who led Seychelles to independence in 1976 and was deposed in the 1977 coup; he returned from exile to help restore multiparty politics.

pullquote.text: An almost bloodless coup turned the young republic into a one-party socialist state for a generation.

eras.title: Democracy and Prosperity
eras.standfirst: A return to multiparty democracy in 1993 underpinned modern Seychelles, a high-income economy built on tourism and tuna.
body: Under domestic and international pressure after the Cold War, René announced a return to pluralism, and a new multiparty constitution was adopted in 1993. Mancham came home from exile to contest the first competitive elections, which René won, beginning a period of democratic competition that has since seen peaceful transfers of power between parties.

Free of single-party rule, Seychelles consolidated one of Africa's most prosperous economies. Tourism, drawing on the islands' beaches and protected natural sites, became the leading sector, accounting for a large share of output and employment, while industrial tuna fishing and canning made Seychelles a major Indian Ocean tuna exporter.

By the early twenty-first century Seychelles had reached high-income status, with per-capita output many times its pre-independence level — among the highest in Africa. The small republic has paired this prosperity with a strong emphasis on marine conservation and the protection of its World Heritage sites.

events.title: Return to multiparty democracy
events.summary: A new constitution restored multiparty politics; the first competitive presidential elections were held, won by René.

events.title: High-income tourism and tuna economy
events.summary: Tourism became the dominant sector and tuna fishing a leading export, lifting Seychelles to high-income status.

figures.name: France-Albert René
figures.blurb: Took power in the 1977 coup and led a one-party socialist state before overseeing the return to multiparty democracy in 1993; he remained president until 2004.

figures.name: James Mancham
figures.blurb: Led Seychelles to independence in 1976 and became its first president; deposed in the 1977 coup, he returned from exile to help restore multiparty democracy.

figures.name: France-Albert René
figures.blurb: Came to power in the 1977 coup and ruled a one-party socialist state for over a decade before presiding over the return to multiparty democracy in 1993.

figures.name: Pierre Poivre
figures.blurb: Promoted Seychelles as a base for spice cultivation in the 1770s to challenge the Dutch monopoly on cloves and nutmeg.
